findings.summary: multi_project_reporter.py hard-imports dashboard_generator without guard, defeating the optional-dep pattern in reporting/__init__.py
evidence: multi_project_reporter.py line 15: `from infrastructure.reporting.dashboard_generator import generate_all_dashboards` — unconditional top-level import with no try/except guard

reporting/__init__.py lines 47-57 wraps the same dashboard_generator import in try/except with `_DASHBOARD_AVAILABLE = True/False`, but multi_project_reporter bypasses that guard entirely with its own direct import

dashboard_generator.py guards matplotlib/numpy/plotly individually (lines 16-30) but the module itself is always importable; generate_all_dashboards (line 3391) calls generate_matplotlib_dashboard unconditionally which will NameError on `plt` when matplotlib is absent
Any caller of generate_multi_project_report() will fail at import time if dashboard_generator cannot be imported on a system without matplotlib/plotly
findings.suggestion: Wrap the import in multi_project_reporter.py in a try/except mirroring the pattern in reporting/__init__.py, and guard the generate_all_dashboards call with a runtime check (e.g. check MATPLOTLIB_AVAILABLE before calling it).

findings.summary: doc_scanner.py suppresses F401 on six imports that are genuinely unused in that file's own scope
evidence: Line 16: `import json  # noqa: F401` — json is not referenced in doc_scanner.py body; it is used inside doc_discovery/doc_accuracy sub-modules imported separately
Line 20: `from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass, field  # noqa: F401` — dataclass/field/asdict not used in this file; ScanResults is imported from doc_models
Line 23: `from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Set, Tuple  # noqa: F401` — mass-import with noqa; Set, Tuple, Optional are unused in the file body
Line 25: `import yaml  # noqa: F401` — yaml is not referenced anywhere in doc_scanner.py

Lines 31-38: `from infrastructure.validation.doc_models import AccuracyIssue, CompletenessGap, DocumentationFile, LinkIssue, QualityIssue, ScanResults  # noqa: F401` — only ScanResults is used directly in this file; the remaining five symbols are silently re-exported with no __all__ declaration
findings.suggestion: Remove imports genuinely unused in this file. If re-exporting doc_models symbols to callers is intentional, declare them in __all__ and document the re-export intent rather than suppressing the linter.

findings.summary: integrity.py applies noqa: F401 to calculate_file_hash which is actually consumed locally, creating misleading re-export signal
evidence: Line 23: `from infrastructure.core.file_operations import calculate_file_hash  # noqa: F401`
calculate_file_hash is used on lines 66 and 624 within the same file — the import is not unused
The noqa comment implies this is a deliberate re-export, misleading future maintainers and suppressing linter checks that would catch a real unused import if the local usages were later removed
findings.suggestion: Remove the `# noqa: F401` comment; the symbol is locally consumed and the suppression is both incorrect and misleading.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/stage_monitor.py is only tested via one shared test file that also covers function_profiler, leaving several branches untested
evidence: Grep for `stage_monitor` across all test files returns exactly one match: tests/infra_tests/core/test_performance.py
stage_monitor.py provides PerformanceMonitor, StagePerformanceTracker, ResourceUsage, get_system_resources, performance_context — a substantial surface directly used by pipeline.py
The psutil-absent code paths in _check_memory/_check_cpu/_check_disk (returning {'psutil_unavailable': True}) are guarded by conditional but have no dedicated tests exercising the None-psutil branch
findings.suggestion: Add dedicated tests for StagePerformanceTracker and performance_context covering both psutil-present and psutil-absent branches using real timing (no mocks).

findings.summary: test_environment.py has three separate test methods that make the identical assertion: check_python_version() returns True
evidence: TestCheckPythonVersion.test_python_version_check_succeeds (line 23): calls check_python_version(), asserts result is True
TestEnvironmentState.test_check_python_version_succeeds_in_test_env (line 110): same call, same assertion, different class name only
TestEnvironmentSetupIntegration.test_full_environment_check (lines 85-93): also calls check_python_version() and asserts True as part of a multi-step test
All three paths exercise the same positive-case branch with no variation in inputs or state
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to one test for the positive case. Add a test for the negative path if the function accepts a configurable minimum version parameter.

findings.summary: Several trivial helper functions in logging_progress.py have multi-paragraph docstrings with Args/Returns/Example sections that exceed the function's complexity
evidence: calculate_eta_ema (lines 29-76): 18-line docstring with Args, Returns, Example block for a 10-line EMA blend function
calculate_eta_with_confidence (lines 79-129): 20-line docstring for a 3-branch linear extrapolation
log_progress_bar (lines 132-163): 13-line docstring including a terminal-output Example for a function that builds a filled/empty block string
log_with_spinner (lines 246-287): docstring includes a full terminal-output Example for a straightforward try/finally context manager
Pattern is consistent: every function regardless of complexity receives the identical docstring scaffolding structure
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on sub-10-line helpers to a single summary line plus Args/Returns only. Reserve Example blocks for genuinely non-obvious behaviors or primary public API.

findings.summary: _check_network() in health_check.py makes live DNS and TCP calls to hard-coded external hosts (google.com, 1.1.1.1) in a production health check
evidence: Lines 151-155: `socket.gethostbyname('google.com')` — live DNS resolution against a hard-coded external host
Lines 158-160: `socket.create_connection(('1.1.1.1', 80), timeout=5)` — live TCP connection to Cloudflare with a 5-second timeout per call
get_health_status() iterates all self.checks including 'network', so every call to is_healthy() or get_metrics() makes two external network calls
This creates a fragility point in offline environments, CI runners behind firewalls, and any air-gapped deployment
findings.suggestion: Accept network test hosts as constructor parameters with the current values as defaults. Alternatively, make _check_network opt-in rather than running automatically in every get_health_status() call.

findings.summary: dashboard_generator.py exceeds 3400 lines, combining matplotlib, plotly, health radar, pipeline efficiency, codebase analysis, and output analysis into a single flat module
evidence: generate_all_dashboards is defined at line 3391, placing the file at 3400+ lines minimum

The file independently handles: test count charts, coverage charts, health radar charts, project breakdowns, pipeline efficiency, pipeline bottleneck analysis, output distribution, codebase complexity, plotly interactive dashboards, and CSV exports
These are at least six distinct visual sub-domains, each with separate data shapes and rendering strategies
infrastructure/reporting/ already has 14 sibling Python files; dashboard_generator is an outlier by at least an order of magnitude
findings.suggestion: Split into focused sub-modules under infrastructure/reporting/dashboard/: e.g., matplotlib_charts.py, plotly_charts.py, health_charts.py, pipeline_charts.py. Expose the same public surface from a dashboard/__init__.py facade.

findings.summary: Logging is split across five infrastructure/core modules where two (logging_constants, logging_helpers) are too small to justify independent module status
evidence: logging_utils.py imports from all four sibling modules (lines 24-28) and re-exports format_duration and format_error_with_suggestions in __all__
logging_constants.py is 32 lines — a dict and two booleans; logging_helpers.py is 55 lines — two utility functions

All 13+ reporting modules import exclusively via `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger`, meaning callers see only logging_utils and the split adds navigation cost with no cohesion gain
logging_constants.py docstring notes it exists to 'avoid circular import issues' — the split is a workaround, not a domain boundary
findings.suggestion: Merge logging_constants.py (32 lines) and logging_helpers.py (55 lines) back into logging_utils.py. The circular-import risk they solve is eliminated if the constants are placed above the functions that use them within the same file. Keep logging_formatters.py and logging_progress.py separate only if they have callers outside logging_utils.
